reasoning: Q3 is critically underperforming at 3621ms vs. DuckDB's 91.7ms (39x gap). Three critical fixes are identified and MUST be applied: (1) Thread parallelism on 64 cores (40-45x speedup potential), (2) Hash aggregation (O(1) vs. O(log n)), (3) Top-K heap instead of full sort. These are fundamental implementation gaps, not optional enhancements. Combined impact expected: ~80ms, closing the gap to competitive performance.
focus_areas: Implement morsel-driven thread parallelism across customer filter, orders join, and lineitem join phases
Replace std::map aggregation with unordered_map or custom hash table for O(1) insertion
Eliminate full sort of 2M groups—use min-heap for Top-10 selection during iteration
strategy_notes: All three critical fixes target cpu_bound bottlenecks and are independent—implement simultaneously. (1) Thread Parallelism: Divide each table into ~100K-row morsels, use thread pool (32-48 threads), maintain thread-local customer sets, orders maps, and aggregates, merge in final phase. (2) Hash Aggregation: Precompute fast FNV-1a hash for {orderkey, orderdate, shippriority} composite key, switch to unordered_map or hand-rolled open-addressing table with ~2.7M preallocated slots (75% load factor). (3) Top-K: Maintain priority_queue<ResultRow> (min-heap on revenue DESC) during lineitem iteration, cap at 10 elements, extract sorted results at end. Risk profile is medium-low (all well-established techniques). Expected outcome: reduce 3621ms to ~80ms, achieving DuckDB-competitive performance.
notes: Iteration 1 of 3. The optimization history is empty (iter_0 is baseline). Be aggressive: all three fixes target high-impact bottlenecks and must ship together to achieve meaningful speedup. Do not defer—remaining 2 iterations are for secondary optimizations (SIMD, column pruning, zone maps) if needed. Correctness verified; focus is pure performance. Hardware: 64-core Xeon, L3 cache size allows morsel sizes of 100K+ rows per thread.
